literal: an indicative mark, a secondary characteristic; upa- + lakṣaṇa 'mark, defining sign'
invariant_definition: *Upalakṣaṇa* names an indicative or extrinsic mark used to pick out a referent without itself belonging to the referent's *svarūpa*. The standard example: when a house is identified by the crow sitting on its roof ('the house with the crow'), the crow is *upalakṣaṇa* — a contingent feature used for identification, not part of what the house is. The pair contrast is with *viśeṣaṇa* (qualifier) and with *svarūpa-lakṣaṇa* (intrinsic defining mark). *Upalakṣaṇa* matters enormously in Vedāntic exegesis because so much of the dispute over Upaniṣadic identity-statements turns on whether predicates of Brahman are *svarūpa-lakṣaṇa* (essential), *viśeṣaṇa* (qualifying), or *upalakṣaṇa* (merely indicative). Advaitins are systematic users of the category: predicates such as 'creator,' 'preserver,' 'destroyer' are *taṭastha-lakṣaṇa* of Brahman, functioning as *upalakṣaṇa*-style markers rather than constituting Brahman's *svarūpa*.

per_school.definition: Predicates that apply to Brahman through its *vyāvahārika* role (cause of the world, *Īśvara*, etc.) are *taṭastha-lakṣaṇa* and function as *upalakṣaṇa*. *Sat-cit-ānanda* is, by contrast, *svarūpa-lakṣaṇa*. The distinction is the principal exegetical tool by which Advaita reads cosmological predicates of Brahman without taking them as essential constituents.

Rāmānuja resists the Advaitic move that turns cosmological predicates into *upalakṣaṇa*. For him, predicates of Bhagavān are *viśeṣaṇas* — real qualifiers — because Bhagavān is *saviśeṣa*: the predicates belong to him intrinsically as the substantive who has *cit* and *acit* as body. *Upalakṣaṇa* is therefore restricted to genuinely contingent marks.

*Upalakṣaṇa* is a working tool in ritual hermeneutics: a procedural mark sometimes indicates a class of rites without itself being a part of the procedure. The grammar of *upalakṣaṇa* in Mīmāṃsā exegesis is one of the historical sources for the Vedāntic deployment of the same distinction.
primary_loci: Mīmāṃsā-Sūtra commentaries on viṣaya-vākya [NOT YET RETRIEVED]

per_school.definition: In grammatical analysis *upalakṣaṇa* names a generalizing use of a particular instance: a stated rule or example *upalakṣayati* a wider class without being limited to the literal case. The technique is sometimes invoked when commentators justify extending a sūtra beyond its literal scope.
primary_loci: Mahābhāṣya passages on upalakṣaṇa [NOT YET RETRIEVED]

translator_note: 'Indicative mark' or 'extrinsic mark' is acceptable. Where Advaita is using *upalakṣaṇa* against *viśeṣaṇa* exegetically, the technical pair should be preserved in Sanskrit; English 'qualifier' vs 'identifier' can stand in but should be glossed with the originals.
